spans.text: Fig. 2: The relative amounts of different particles and antiparticles produced at RHIC in the
collisions of Gold nuclei with energies sNN = 200 GeV per nucleon-nucleon collision. All
the measurements (red points) agree very well with a simple thermal model (blue lines) with
an effective temperature around 1.6 trillion degrees, in line with theoretical calculations,
and a net nucleon (quark) density that is lower than in previous, lower-energy experiments.
The inset shows that the fraction
! of strange quarks has saturated at the same density as up
and down quarks. Figure taken from refs. [3,23].
The total number of heavy-ion collisions created at the LHC will be comparable to
the total number of heavy ion collisions created at RHIC. The LHC will be at least as
hot a furnace as RHIC, in the sense that the systems produced in heavy-ion
collisions at the LHC will have an effective temperature that is similar to that
produced at RHIC. This is one factor that makes strangelet production no more
likely at the LHC than at RHIC. Another major factor pointing in the same direction
is that the net density of nucleons, measured by the baryon number, will be lower
at the LHC than at RHIC. This is because the system produced in heavy-ion
collisions at the LHC is spread over a larger rapidity range, and the same total net
baryon number will be spread over a larger volume. As discussed in more detail in
the Appendix, this effect has already been seen at RHIC, where the net density of
nucleons is lower than in lower-energy experiments, and this trend will continue at
the LHC [3]. Since strangelets require baryon number to be formed, this effect
makes strangelet production less likely at the LHC than at RHIC.
We conclude on general physical grounds that heavy-ion collisions at the LHC are
less likely to produce strangelets than the lower-energy heavy-ion collisions already
carried out in recent years at RHIC, just as strangelet production at RHIC was less
likely than in previous lower-energy experiments carried out in the 1980s and 1990s
Knowing that strangelet production at the LHC is less likely than at previous lower-

chunks.title: Review of the Safety of LHC Collisions

spans.text: of microscopic black holes at the LHC. For the cases that were already considered in
previous reports, it confirms their conclusions that there is absolutely no danger. In
addition, Section 4 reviews the highly hypothetical production of neutral stable black
holes, and the possibility that these black holes could be trapped inside the Earth. While
such a possibility is dismissed by most of the experts, it is conceivable, and it could have
potentially dangerous consequences if these highly hypothetical, neutral, stable
microscopic black holes could accrete matter on Earth on time scales shorter than the
natural lifetime of the solar system. For this to occur, a number of increasingly unlikely
conditions should be satisfied: 1) the fundamental scale of gravity should be within the
LHC energy range, possible only if extra spatial dimensions with very peculiar
properties do exist; 2) Hawking radiation, and more general arguments for the decay of
any black holes produced at the LHC, should fail; 3) the Schwinger mechanism, by which
a charged black hole becomes electrically neutral via particle-antiparticle creation near
the horizon, should still work, even though it is based on physical principles very close to
those underlying Hawking radiation.
Going beyond the RHIC and LSSG reports, the above-mentioned possibility is now
directly addressed by the LSAG report, on the basis of the GM paper, which is the most
detailed and specialized presentation of all the scientific evidence we have on this issue.
The GM paper admits from the start the possibility that compelling but not
experimentally tested mechanisms, such as Hawking radiation, could be suppressed or
absent. It chooses instead a conservative or "worst-case" scenario at every instance where
an experimental or theoretical uncertainty is encountered. It then uses observational data
on cosmic rays and on astronomical objects such as the Sun or compact stars to exclude
any danger at the LHC.
In particular, at the LHC energy, any danger for the Earth on time scales lower than or
comparable to the natural lifetime of the solar system can be ruled out on the basis of its
contradiction with the observation of white dwarf stars of known mass, age and other
properties. This conclusion, while entirely valid for the LHC, would need further work to
be extended to conceivable future colliders of much higher energies. A powerful
argument applicable also to higher energies is formulated making reference to observed
neutron stars, but this argument relies on properties of cosmic rays and neutrinos that,
while highly plausible, do require confirmation, as can be expected in the coming years.
Table 2: Black hole production rates, per million years, induced by cosmic rays impinging
on a R = 5400 km white dwarf. Np refers to the case of 100% proton composition, NF e
refers to 100% Fe. MD = Mmin /3 and y = Mmin /14 TeV.
hole mass to be Mmin = 3MD ; for example, [5] used as a benchmark the less-conservative
value M = 5MD .
The resulting cross sections are then convoluted with the CTEQ6M [54] parton distribu-
tion functions. The resulting nucleon cross sections are, in turn, convolved with the measured
ultrahigh-energy cosmic ray flux, extracted from the latest Auger spectra [40]. In the case of
cosmic ray primaries that are nuclei of mass number A, one must also use a nucleon energy
reduced by this factor. The rate calculations of Appendix E are carried out for two test
cases: that of incident protons, and that of incident iron nuclei (A=56). Partial results are
summarized in table 2, for production on a white dwarf of radius 5400 km, and more results
are presented in Appendix E. (In that Appendix we also present figures resulting from a
20% hypothesized overestimate of cosmic ray energies, to model the quoted Auger energy
resolution of ±20% [40, 55]. )
According to the stopping calculations of section 5, not all cosmic-ray produced black
holes are stopped by a white dwarf. In order to allow for sufficient column density, we must
require the cosmic rays to reach the white dwarf at an angle sufficiently close to the azimuth
as to force the black hole to traverse a sufficient fraction of the full column density of the star.
The angular dependence of the column density is shown in the right plot of fig. 1. In the
case of D = 5, our calculated column densities required for stopping are a mere few percent
of those available and we can therefore easily accept a reduction to 10% of the available
column density. In the cases of D = 6 and 7, to be conservative we can allow for at least
30% and 80% of the full available column density, leading to a reduction of the useful cosmic
ray flux to a level of 10% and 2%, respectively. Multiplying these efficiencies by the number
of events given in table 2, and integrating over a period of 10 million years, leads to total
numbers of accumulated black holes larger than 5000, even in the totally extreme case of

chunks.title: Astrophysical implications of hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes

spans.text: mBHs produced at a collider can be captured by Earth and accrete at the
12  In a previous paper[21] Giddings wrote: “For masses of order the fundamental
Planck scale [i.e. M5 ] there is no control over quantum gravity effects which are
likely to invalidate the semiclassical ... picture.”
13 This is a real concern because the “safety critical” black holes in theories with
2 extra dimensions cannot be excluded anymore if their scattering cross section is
smaller by less than a mere factor 10 than semiclassically expected (see fig.2 of G
Eddington limit. Thereby they might emit Hawking radiation that might be
dangerous to Earth as a whole or the inhabitants of CERN and its surround-
ings. The astrophysical argument by G & M[22] and Koch et al.[31] does not
exclude this scenario, because it allows for lifetimes of white dwarfs and neu-
tron stars with Eddington accreting mBHs (section 3) far exceeding the age of
the universe. Such stars could still exist, even if they harboured a microscopic
accreting black hole since the dawn of time.
Moreover - in section 4 - I outlined another independent gap in the “astro-
physical exclusion” of potentially dangerous mBHs.
Thus, at the present stage of knowledge there is a definite residual risk from
mBH production at colliders. This final conclusion differs from the one drawn
by G & M. This is not because of any disagreement over any specific conclu-
sion of their excellent paper. Rather the difference is the sole result of either
employing the alternative, physically plausible scenario 3 for the physics of
mBHs or including parameter regions in which mBHs are not expected to be
well described by a semiclassical approximation of quantum gravity into the
safety analysis.
5.2 Proposal for risk mitigating measures

chunks.title: On the potential catastrophic risk from metastable quantum-black holes produced at particle colliders

spans.text: giddings@physics.ucsb.edu
michelangelo.mangano@cern.ch
The recent paper [1] claims to produce a scenario for LHC risk that is not excluded by
our previous work[2], or by the paper [3] supporting the same conclusions. This proposal is
based on an idea, extrapolating claims in [4], that Hawking radiation is suppressed until a
certain mass threshold, which [1] proposes occurs for black hole radii comparable to the scale
where higher-dimensional gravity matches onto four-dimensional gravity. Ref. [1] claims that
once the Hawking radiation turns on, the power output would be at dangerous levels like
1016 W, and that moreover such a scenario is not excluded by astrophysical constraints such
as those derived in [2] from white dwarfs and neutron stars.
To assess this scenario, let us begin by observing that a universal relation for the energy
output of Hawking radiation is of the form
dt     3840π R  2                  R
where D is the spacetime dimension, R is the Schwarzschild radius, and ξ is an O(1) gray-
body factor parameterizing the deviations from a precise blackbody spectrum. This general
formula follows from two facts: the Stefan-Boltzmann law, and the formula for the Hawking
temperature of the black hole,
4π R
Moreover, this formula agrees with eq. (2) of [1].
The proposal of [1] is that Hawking radiation is suppressed compared to this usual Hawk-
ing result (hence the black hole is “metastable”) until the black hole reaches the radius scale
R ∼ L, where L is comparable to the scale of transition to four-dimensional behavior. (This
could be anywhere in the range between the parameters RD and RC introduced in [2].) When
it reaches this scale, the usual Hawking radiation is then claimed to switch on. Ref. [1] con-
siders in particular scales near L ∼ 10−5 cm, where one is claimed to find the large power
output stated above.
However, using these parameters one readily finds from the formula (1) a negligible power
output of size

coverage_backfill.warning: Some source chunks were skipped by budget. The runner reports coverage risk but does not insert deterministic backfill claims into the map.

decision_question: Why did investigators conclude that LHC operation would not create a catastrophic black hole risk?

proposal.claim: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) produces collision energies that are significantly lower than those reached by high-energy cosmic rays bombarding the Earth and other astronomical bodies.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: LHC design collision energy per beam
claim_quantities.source_quote: designed to collide pairs of protons each with energies of 7 TeV

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Energy threshold for cosmic rays exceeding LHC energy
claim_quantities.source_quote: if its energy reaches or exceeds 10^8 GeV, i.e., 10^17 eV

proposal.importance_rationale: This provides the fundamental empirical basis for safety: if nature has tolerated higher energies without catastrophe, the LHC is inherently safer.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This provides the fundamental empirical basis for safety: if nature has tolerated higher energies without catastrophe, the LHC is inherently safer.
proposal.source_quote: The energies of accelerators have been increasing regularly over the past decades, though they are still far below those of the most energetic cosmic rays.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: LHC design collision energy per beam
claim_quantities.source_quote: designed to collide pairs of protons each with energies of 7 TeV

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Energy threshold for cosmic rays exceeding LHC energy
claim_quantities.source_quote: if its energy reaches or exceeds 10^8 GeV, i.e., 10^17 eV

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: LHC collision energies are much lower than those of cosmic rays that have bombarded Earth for billions of years.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: The energies of accelerators have been increasing regularly over the past decades, though they are still far below those of the most energetic cosmic rays.

proposal.claim: Microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay via Hawking radiation before reaching detector walls, and even if stable, they would not accrete matter in a dangerous manner.
proposal.importance_rationale: Addresses the specific concern of black hole production and their potential for runaway growth.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Addresses the specific concern of black hole production and their potential for runaway growth.
proposal.source_quote: Any microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay by Hawking radiation before they reach the detector walls.

stated_limitations: requires violation of basic physical principles for stability/suppressed decay

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Microscopic black holes would decay quickly or, if stable, lack the mechanism to grow dangerously.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: Any microscopic black holes produced at the LHC are expected to decay by Hawking radiation before they reach the detector walls.
the decay rate would be directly related to the production rate, and the expected lifetime would be very short.

proposal.claim: The continued existence of the Earth and other astronomical bodies (like the Sun and Moon) provides empirical evidence that high-energy collisions do not produce dangerous vacuum bubbles or magnetic monopoles.
proposal.importance_rationale: Uses the survival of celestial bodies as a 'natural experiment' to rule out catastrophic vacuum decay or monopole catalysis.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Uses the survival of celestial bodies as a 'natural experiment' to rule out catastrophic vacuum decay or monopole catalysis.
proposal.source_quote: The continued existence of the Universe means that such vacuum bubbles are not produced in cosmic-ray collisions, and hence the LHC will also not produce any vacuum bubbles.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The Earth and Sun have survived billions of years of cosmic-ray bombardment, proving that such collisions do not trigger vacuum decay or dangerous magnetic monopoles.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The LHC's operation presents no conceivable threat because the energies achieved are significantly lower than those observed in cosmic-ray collisions with astronomical bodies, which have remained stable for billions of years.

supporting_quotes.quote: The continued existence of the Universe means that such vacuum bubbles are not produced in cosmic-ray collisions, and hence the LHC will also not produce any vacuum bubbles.

The continued existence of the Earth and other astronomical bodies after billions of years of high-energy cosmic-ray bombardment means that any monopoles produced could not catalyze proton decay at any appreciable rate.

proposal.claim: The existence of Earth and other astronomical bodies for billions of years despite continuous exposure to high-energy cosmic rays proves that any processes occurring at or above LHC energies (including vacuum bubbles and magnetic monopoles) are not catastrophic.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Comparison of historical cosmic ray hits vs. projected LHC collisions
claim_quantities.measures: times the total number of collisions expected over the duration of LHC experiments
claim_quantities.source_quote: such energetic LHC-like cosmic rays have already hit the Earth (or the Sun) in numbers equivalent to one hundred thousand (or one billion) times the total number of collisions expected over the duration of LHC experiments.

proposal.importance_rationale: This provides the fundamental safety proof by comparing LHC conditions to naturally occurring, non-catastrophic cosmic ray events.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This provides the fundamental safety proof by comparing LHC conditions to naturally occurring, non-catastrophic cosmic ray events.
proposal.source_quote: the hypothesis that the LHC operation would threaten the existence of the Earth is irreconcilable with the fact that the Earth, the Sun and other objects in the Cosmos have persisted for billions of years in spite of their continuous exposure to energetic cosmic rays.

claim_context.evidence_design: Comparative observational analysis of cosmic ray flux vs. planetary age
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Catastrophic vacuum instability or magnetic monopole production
claim_context.population: Earth, Sun, and other astronomical bodies
claim_context.stated_dose_or_threshold: Energies equivalent to or larger than those of LHC collisions

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Comparison of historical cosmic ray hits vs. projected LHC collisions
claim_quantities.measures: times the total number of collisions expected over the duration of LHC experiments
claim_quantities.source_quote: such energetic LHC-like cosmic rays have already hit the Earth (or the Sun) in numbers equivalent to one hundred thousand (or one billion) times the total number of collisions expected over the duration of LHC experiments.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The fact that Earth and the Sun have survived billions of years of high-energy cosmic ray bombardment proves that such collisions do not cause catastrophic vacuum instability or produce dangerous magnetic monopoles.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The SPC endorses the LSAG report's conclusion that there is no danger from the production of new particles or states of matter (magnetic monopoles, vacuum bubbles, strangelets, and black holes) at the LHC, based on observational evidence of cosmic ray collisions with celestial bodies.

supporting_quotes.quote: the hypothesis that the LHC operation would threaten the existence of the Earth is irreconcilable with the fact that the Earth, the Sun and other objects in the Cosmos have persisted for billions of years in spite of their continuous exposure to energetic cosmic rays.

proposal.claim: The production of microscopic black holes at the LHC is ruled out as a danger because any such black holes would have to satisfy an extremely unlikely set of conditions (e.g., specific extra dimensions, failure of Hawking radiation) and are contradicted by the observed properties of white dwarf stars.
proposal.importance_rationale: Directly addresses the specific concern regarding black hole accretion and stability.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Directly addresses the specific concern regarding black hole accretion and stability.
proposal.source_quote: any danger for the Earth on time scales lower than or comparable to the natural lifetime of the solar system can be ruled out on the basis of its contradiction with the observation of white dwarf stars of known mass, age and other properties.

claim_context.evidence_design: Theoretical analysis and observational data (GM paper)
claim_context.stated_dose_or_threshold: Time scales lower than or comparable to the natural lifetime of the solar system
stated_limitations: requires extra spatial dimensions with very peculiar properties

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Black hole production is ruled out because it requires highly improbable physical conditions that contradict observed astronomical data like white dwarf properties.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The SPC endorses the LSAG report's conclusion that there is no danger from the production of new particles or states of matter (magnetic monopoles, vacuum bubbles, strangelets, and black holes) at the LHC, based on observational evidence of cosmic ray collisions with celestial bodies.

supporting_quotes.quote: any danger for the Earth on time scales lower than or comparable to the natural lifetime of the solar system can be ruled out on the basis of its contradiction with the observation of white dwarf stars of known mass, age and other properties.

proposal.claim: Strangelet production at LHC energies is less likely than at lower RHIC energies, and there is no evidence of strangelet-induced conversion of matter from high-energy cosmic rays bombarding the Moon.
proposal.importance_rationale: Addresses the concern of 'strange' matter conversion, a distinct category of exotic particle risk.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Addresses the concern of 'strange' matter conversion, a distinct category of exotic particle risk.
proposal.source_quote: the data from the RHIC program strongly reinforce the arguments collected at the start of the RHIC program: there is no conceivable danger from strangelet production at LHC energy.

claim_context.evidence_design: Comparison of RHIC program results and cosmic ray data
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Coalescence with normal matter to catalyze conversion of the world into strange matter

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Strangelet production is less likely at LHC energies than at lower RHIC energies, and no evidence exists for strangelet-related events in cosmic ray data.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The SPC endorses the LSAG report's conclusion that there is no danger from the production of new particles or states of matter (magnetic monopoles, vacuum bubbles, strangelets, and black holes) at the LHC, based on observational evidence of cosmic ray collisions with celestial bodies.

supporting_quotes.quote: the data from the RHIC program strongly reinforce the arguments collected at the start of the RHIC program: there is no conceivable danger from strangelet production at LHC energy.

proposal.claim: Stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a risk to Earth on timescales shorter than the Earth's natural lifetime.
proposal.importance_rationale: This is the primary conclusion regarding the safety of LHC operations concerning black hole production.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This is the primary conclusion regarding the safety of LHC operations concerning black hole production.
proposal.source_quote: this study finds no basis for concerns that TeV-scale black holes from the LHC could pose a risk to Earth on time scales shorter than the Earth’s natural lifetime.

claim_context.evidence_design: Theoretical analysis and conservative calculations based on known physical laws
claim_context.exposure_or_option: LHC operation producing stable TeV-scale black holes
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Risk to Earth on timescales shorter than its natural lifetime

must_preserve_terms: no risk of any significance whatsoever
whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: LHC-produced stable black holes pose no significant risk to Earth's survival over its natural lifetime.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The study concludes that hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a catastrophic risk to Earth because their accretion rates are insufficient to cause macroscopic growth on timescales shorter than the solar system's lifetime, and any such scenarios are further constrained by the observed stability of white dwarfs and neutron stars.

supporting_quotes.quote: this study finds no basis for concerns that TeV-scale black holes from the LHC could pose a risk to Earth on time scales shorter than the Earth’s natural lifetime.

conservative arguments based on detailed calculations and the best-available scientific knowledge, including solid astronomical data, conclude, from multiple perspectives, that there is no risk of any significance whatsoever from such black holes.

proposal.claim: In scenarios where the crossover radius (RC) exceeds 200 Å, stable black holes cannot exist for a crossover radius greater than ~15 Å due to observational data on white dwarf and neutron star lifetimes.
proposal.importance_rationale: This provides a secondary bound based on the observed stability of other astronomical objects.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This provides a secondary bound based on the observed stability of other astronomical objects.
proposal.source_quote: In this case, however, examination of the latter dense-star scenario then produces an argument that, given observational data setting the lifetimes of such objects at a billion years or more, such stable black holes cannot in fact exist for a crossover radius greater than ≃ 15Å.

claim_context.evidence_design: Comparison of accretion models to observed stellar lifetimes
claim_context.exposure_or_option: Stable black holes with RC > 200 Å
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Existence of stable black holes for large RC

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Even in cases where growth might be faster, observational data from dense stars rules out large crossover radii.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The study concludes that hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a catastrophic risk to Earth because their accretion rates are insufficient to cause macroscopic growth on timescales shorter than the solar system's lifetime, and any such scenarios are further constrained by the observed stability of white dwarfs and neutron stars.

supporting_quotes.quote: In this case, however, examination of the latter dense-star scenario then produces an argument that, given observational data setting the lifetimes of such objects at a billion years or more, such stable black holes cannot in fact exist for a crossover radius greater than ≃ 15Å.

proposal.claim: Charged TeV-scale black holes would stop in the Earth for masses up to about 7 TeV, and in the Sun if heavier.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum mass for Earth stopping power

proposal.importance_rationale: This addresses the specific scenario of charged black holes, which would be subject to electromagnetic stopping power.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This addresses the specific scenario of charged black holes, which would be subject to electromagnetic stopping power.
proposal.source_quote: the Earth provides enough stopping power for black holes with unit electric charge up to a mass of the order of 7 TeV.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum mass for Earth stopping power

supporting_quotes.quote: the Earth provides enough stopping power for black holes with unit electric charge up to a mass of the order of 7 TeV.

proposal.claim: The accretion of a white dwarf by a captured black hole would occur on time scales much shorter than the observed lifetime of the white dwarf.
proposal.importance_rationale: This provides a secondary safety check: if black holes were produced, they would have already destroyed the white dwarf by now.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This provides a secondary safety check: if black holes were produced, they would have already destroyed the white dwarf by now.
proposal.source_quote: the implication of these arguments is that such scenarios, where Earth could be disrupted on time scales short as compared to its natural lifetime, are ruled out.

claim_context.evidence_design: Comparison of accretion rates to observed stellar ages

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Accretion of a white dwarf occurs on timescales much shorter than its observed lifetime, ruling out certain high-growth scenarios.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The study concludes that hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a catastrophic risk to Earth because their accretion rates are insufficient to cause macroscopic growth on timescales shorter than the solar system's lifetime, and any such scenarios are further constrained by the observed stability of white dwarfs and neutron stars.

supporting_quotes.quote: the implication of these arguments is that such scenarios, where Earth could be disrupted on time scales short as compared to its natural lifetime, are ruled out.

In particular, we have argued that in such scenarios cosmic rays will produce black holes on such astronomical objects, and that these objects will stop even these very high-momentum black holes. We then studied accretion, showing that accreting black holes will disrupt such objects on time scales short as compared to their observed lifetimes.

proposal.claim: Neutron stars provide a robust bound because they can promptly slow down and capture black holes produced by cosmic rays.
proposal.importance_rationale: Neutron stars provide a high-density environment that acts as a 'trap' for black holes, providing an additional layer of safety.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Neutron stars provide a high-density environment that acts as a 'trap' for black holes, providing an additional layer of safety.
proposal.source_quote: the accretion times are very rapid compared to the neutron star’s lifetime, O(Gyr)

claim_context.evidence_design: Analysis of stopping power in high-density environments
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Capture and slowing of black holes

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Neutron stars can rapidly slow down and capture cosmic-ray produced black holes due to their extreme density.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The study concludes that hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a catastrophic risk to Earth because their accretion rates are insufficient to cause macroscopic growth on timescales shorter than the solar system's lifetime, and any such scenarios are further constrained by the observed stability of white dwarfs and neutron stars.

supporting_quotes.quote: the accretion times are very rapid compared to the neutron star’s lifetime, O(Gyr)

proposal.claim: The probability of an LHC-produced black hole being trapped by Earth's gravity is low, as the velocity spectrum of produced black holes typically exceeds the escape velocity.
proposal.importance_rationale: This addresses the initial capture of a black hole, which is a prerequisite for any subsequent accretion risk.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This addresses the initial capture of a black hole, which is a prerequisite for any subsequent accretion risk.
proposal.source_quote: As soon as M & 7 TeV the expected number of trapped black holes falls below 1 even with the looser inelasticity value of y = 0.7.

claim_context.evidence_design: Analysis of production kinematics and escape velocity

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Most LHC-produced black holes will have velocities high enough to escape Earth's gravity.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The study concludes that hypothetical stable TeV-scale black holes produced at the LHC would not pose a catastrophic risk to Earth because their accretion rates are insufficient to cause macroscopic growth on timescales shorter than the solar system's lifetime, and any such scenarios are further constrained by the observed stability of white dwarfs and neutron stars.

supporting_quotes.quote: As soon as M & 7 TeV the expected number of trapped black holes falls below 1 even with the looser inelasticity value of y = 0.7.

proposal.claim: Quasistable mBHs in Scenario 3 can accrete matter at the Eddington limit, potentially leading to catastrophic global heating and seismic activity.

claim_quantities.source_quote: The power of 5.2 x 10^16 W is 1300 times larger than the total geothermal power emitted by Earth

proposal.importance_rationale: This identifies the specific mechanism of harm (Eddington-limited accretion) and the resulting catastrophic consequences.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This identifies the specific mechanism of harm (Eddington-limited accretion) and the resulting catastrophic consequences.
proposal.source_quote: the mBHs accretes at the 5-dimensional Eddington limit. All accreted mass is then reradiated, and the mBH’s mass remains constant on average.

claim_quantities.source_quote: The power of 5.2 x 10^16 W is 1300 times larger than the total geothermal power emitted by Earth

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: If mBHs accrete at the Eddington limit, they could cause global heating and massive earthquakes.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The author argues that a third scenario (Scenario 3) for microscopic black holes (mBHs) allows for metastable, long-lived objects that could accrete matter at the Eddington limit and emit intense Hawking radiation, posing a potential catastrophic risk from collider production that is not ruled out by current astrophysical arguments.

supporting_quotes.quote: the mBHs accretes at the 5-dimensional Eddington limit. All accreted mass is then reradiated, and the mBH’s mass remains constant on average.
eventually catastrophic consequences due to global heating on an unprecedented scale and global-scale earth-quakes would seem certain.

proposal.claim: The astrophysical argument that mBHs would have consumed white dwarfs or neutron stars is invalid for Scenario 3 because their growth is eventually stopped by Hawking radiation.
proposal.importance_rationale: This directly addresses why the standard safety argument (astrophysical exclusion) fails to rule out the risk.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This directly addresses why the standard safety argument (astrophysical exclusion) fails to rule out the risk.
proposal.source_quote: the astrophysical argument of G & M fails to exclude the existence of mBHs in scenario 3 that are dangerous not because they accrete the whole Earth but because of their intense Hawking radiation.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The existence of old white dwarfs does not rule out dangerous mBHs because radiation limits their growth.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The author argues that a third scenario (Scenario 3) for microscopic black holes (mBHs) allows for metastable, long-lived objects that could accrete matter at the Eddington limit and emit intense Hawking radiation, posing a potential catastrophic risk from collider production that is not ruled out by current astrophysical arguments.

supporting_quotes.quote: the astrophysical argument of G & M fails to exclude the existence of mBHs in scenario 3 that are dangerous not because they accrete the whole Earth but because of their intense Hawking radiation.
their growth by accretion is eventually stopped at intermediate sizes via their emission of Hawking radiation.

proposal.claim: The exclusion of 'dangerous' mBHs by Giddings & Mangano is not definite because it relies on a semiclassical approximation that may be invalid in the quantum gravity regime.
proposal.importance_rationale: This identifies a fundamental flaw in the current safety analysis: it assumes a regime of physics (semiclassical) that may not apply to the smallest, most dangerous black holes.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This identifies a fundamental flaw in the current safety analysis: it assumes a regime of physics (semiclassical) that may not apply to the smallest, most dangerous black holes.
proposal.source_quote: mBHs deep in the “quantum gravity” regime (violating eq.(7)) might have smaller scattering cross section than expected in the semiclassically and escape white dwarfs

claim_context.population: mBHs in the quantum gravity regime

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Current safety arguments are based on an assumption (semiclassical validity) that might be wrong for very small black holes.

proposal.claim: Even if the inconsistency were ignored, the astrophysical constraints from white dwarfs and neutron stars would still apply to the proposed metastable black hole scenario.
proposal.importance_rationale: This provides a secondary independent reason why the proposed risk scenario is not viable, even if the mathematical error were somehow corrected.
proposal.relevance_rationale: This provides a secondary independent reason why the proposed risk scenario is not viable, even if the mathematical error were somehow corrected.
proposal.source_quote: the bounds of that paper would apply even to the suggested scenario of [1].

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Astrophysical constraints on white dwarfs and neutron stars remain valid even for the suggested metastable black hole scenario.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The claimed catastrophic risk from metastable black holes at the LHC is based on a mathematical inconsistency where the author applied 4D mass-radius relations to a regime where they are invalid, resulting in an overestimation of power output by a factor of 10^23.

supporting_quotes.quote: the bounds of that paper would apply even to the suggested scenario of [1].

proposal.claim: The conclusions regarding the safety of LHC collisions remain robust because the original study used conservative 'worst-case' assumptions and was not misquoted by the critic.
proposal.importance_rationale: Confirms that the foundational safety assessment for the LHC remains valid despite the criticisms raised in the recent note.
proposal.relevance_rationale: Confirms that the foundational safety assessment for the LHC remains valid despite the criticisms raised in the recent note.
proposal.source_quote: We conclude that the conclusions of [2] on this subject, as stated there and as referred to in the LHC safety assessment report [7], remain robust.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: The original safety assessments for the LHC remain robust and were not undermined by the critic's misinterpretations.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The claimed catastrophic risk from metastable black holes at the LHC is based on a mathematical inconsistency where the author applied 4D mass-radius relations to a regime where they are invalid, resulting in an overestimation of power output by a factor of 10^23.

supporting_quotes.quote: We conclude that the conclusions of [2] on this subject, as stated there and as referred to in the LHC safety assessment report [7], remain robust.
at each point where we have encountered an uncertainty, we have replaced it by a conservative or “worst case” assumption.
